sourceMovie.title: Mulholland Drive
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Mulholland Drive works as a thriller movie with a point of view—fans quote moments, but they also argue about what the film is actually saying about power, desire, or survival. The thriller and drama blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Lost Highway
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where Mulholland Drive excels in thriller, Lost Highway (1997) doubles down on drama to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: Murder on the Orient Express
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Murder on the Orient Express (1974) is the natural next step after Mulholland Drive: same thriller DNA, different perspective. The tense streak and drama elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: The Lady from Shanghai
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Mulholland Drive who respond to its thriller will find The Lady from Shanghai (1947) immediately familiar. Both films share a drama sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: In a Lonely Place
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Mulholland Drive fans keep landing on In a Lonely Place (1950) — the thriller is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: Dead Again
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Dead Again draws from the same thriller well as Mulholland Drive, channeling drama in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 1991 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: Memento
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Memento (2000) captures the thriller appeal of Mulholland Drive without simply imitating it. The mystery streak running through both films is what puts Memento at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: Nightcrawler
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The thriller appeal of Mulholland Drive — that dark edge, the drama — translates cleanly into Nightcrawler (2014). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: Vertigo
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind Vertigo (1958) clearly understood what makes thriller work — the same quality that keeps Mulholland Drive rewatchable. Expect the same tense rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: Changeling
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Changeling (2008) is the kind of film Mulholland Drive fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The drama overlap is real, but it's the tense emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: Blue Velvet
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Blue Velvet shares Mulholland Drive's gift for thriller — the 1986 film delivers mystery without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

faq.question: What should I watch after Mulholland Drive?
faq.answer: Try Lost Highway, Murder on the Orient Express, The Lady from Shanghai—each shares DNA with Mulholland Drive while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Mulholland Drive?
faq.answer: Mulholland Drive (2001) is best described as Thriller, Drama, Mystery. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Mulholland Drive worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.8/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this thriller lane.
